---
title: "`mk.md` — design notes"
subtitle: "A markdown helper whose **output is markdown source**, not `mo.Html`"
author: "MechanicsKit"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
---

## What this is, said sharply

`mk.md(template)` takes a template string with `{var}` placeholders, looks
the variables up in the calling cell's namespace, formats them as **markdown
source**, and returns an object whose primary representation is that
markdown. Quarto, marimo, Jupyter, and `print()` all receive markdown text
and render it however they want.

The key word is *source*. Not HTML, not a `marimo.Html` blob — markdown.

## How marimo does it (so we know what we're adding to)

Reading `marimo/_output/md.py` directly:

- **`mo.md` does no interpolation.** It receives an already-formatted
  string. The f-string in `mo.md(f"…{x}…")` is evaluated by Python before
  `mo.md` is called. Marimo plays no part in the substitution.
- **Markdown is rendered server-side by `python-markdown`** with a heavy
  `pymdownx` extension stack: tables, `arithmatex` for LaTeX, superfences,
  tasklist, footnotes, iconify, and friends. The result is HTML wrapped in
  `<span class="markdown prose …">`.
- **The returned `_md` object is already dual-protocol.** It implements
  `_repr_markdown_()` returning the raw source, and `_mime_()` returning
  `("text/markdown", …)` — source in non-interactive contexts, rendered
  HTML otherwise. Quarto and Jupyter pick the markdown branch.
- **`__format__` is overridden to return the markdown source**, not the
  HTML. This makes nested `mo.md` calls compose under f-string
  interpolation: `mo.md(f"outer… {inner_mo_md} …outer")` keeps `inner`'s
  markdown intact instead of flattening it to one line of HTML. Worth
  borrowing on our `Markdown` class for the same reason.

So our wrapper is not retro-fitting a missing protocol — `mo.md` already
exports `_repr_markdown_`. What it is retro-fitting is **what gets
interpolated in the first place**.

## Why this is not "just an f-string"

A plain f-string runs Python's `__format__` on each placeholder. For
NumPy that gives you `repr(ndarray)`; the resulting string is then handed
to `mo.md` and stored verbatim in `_markdown_text`. By the time quarto
or marimo's python-markdown sees the source, `[[1 2]\n [3 4]]` is baked
in. There is no second chance.

`mk.md`'s job is to intercept the substitution and run each value through
a **type-aware dispatcher** before the source is finalized:

| Type of `A`       | What `{A}` becomes                                    |
|-------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| `int`, `float`, `str` | `str(A)` — same as f-string                       |
| `LatexArray`      | the LaTeX source from `_repr_latex_` (already `$$…$$`) |
| `LatexExpression` | the LaTeX source, wrappable via format spec `{A:math}` |
| `numpy.ndarray` (2-D, ≤ 8×8) | markdown table via `tabulate`                |
| `numpy.ndarray` (2-D, > 8×8) | truncated LaTeX `bmatrix` (matches `LatexArray`'s 8×8 truncation) |
| `pandas.DataFrame`| `df.to_markdown()` — markdown table (`tabulate`)     |
| `list[list]` / `list[dict]` | markdown table via `tabulate`              |
| `marimo.Html` / UI | passed through verbatim (whatever its `__format__` returns) |

Figures (matplotlib, plotly) are deliberately out of scope: they belong in
their own cell or quarto chunk so quarto can attach captions, cross-refs,
and sizing. Inlining base64 PNGs in markdown defeats that.

The user writes one line. The wrapper picks the right markdown
serialization. The renderer (marimo's python-markdown, quarto's pandoc)
renders the resulting markdown. There is no equivalent f-string idiom.

A second, smaller win: dropping the `f` prefix and the `mo.md(...)`
boilerplate. The frame walk that enables it is needed for the dispatcher
anyway, so it costs nothing extra.

## Concrete examples

### Real example: a parameter table

This is the kind of cell `mk.md` exists for. Today, written with `mo.md`
and an f-string:

```python
mo.md(f"""
| Beteckning   | Beskrivning                        | Värde                  | Enhet      |
|:---          |:---                                |:---                    |:---        |
| $d$          | Skruvgäng metrisk diameter         | M{skruvStandard.value} | M standard |
| $P$          | Gängstigning grov                  | {P}                    | mm         |
| $F_{{st}}$   | Sträckkraft $R_{{p02}} \\cdot A_s$ | {F_st/1000:0.2f}       | kN         |
| $M_{{åt}}$   | Åtdragningsmoment                  | {M_åt:0.2f}            | Nm         |
""")
```

The same cell with `mk.md` is identical apart from the prefix:

```python
mk.md("""
| Beteckning   | Beskrivning                        | Värde                  | Enhet      |
|:---          |:---                                |:---                    |:---        |
| $d$          | Skruvgäng metrisk diameter         | M{skruvStandard.value} | M standard |
| $P$          | Gängstigning grov                  | {P}                    | mm         |
| $F_{{st}}$   | Sträckkraft $R_{{p02}} \\cdot A_s$ | {F_st/1000:0.2f}       | kN         |
| $M_{{åt}}$   | Åtdragningsmoment                  | {M_åt:0.2f}            | Nm         |
""")
```

That is the floor of what the wrapper does on a cell like this: drop the
`f`, drop the `mo.md(...)`. The ceiling — type-aware rendering — kicks in
the moment one of those `{...}` resolves to an `ndarray`, a `LatexArray`,
or a `DataFrame` instead of a scalar.

**Important consequence for the implementation.** This example contains
`{F_st/1000:0.2f}` — arithmetic inside the placeholder. Vanilla `str.format`
does **not** support that; it allows only `name`, `name.attr`, and
`name[key]`. To keep this example working without a rewrite, our
`string.Formatter` subclass has to override `get_field` and `eval` the
field text as a Python expression in the caller's namespace. That is
deferred-f-string semantics, and it is the price of "no `f` prefix":

```python
class _MkFormatter(string.Formatter):
    def __init__(self, ns):
        self._ns = ns
    def get_field(self, field_name, args, kwargs):
        return eval(field_name, self._ns), field_name
```

`eval` here is fine — it runs in the cell's own namespace, which the user
already controls.

### A matrix and its eigenvalues

```python
K = np.array([[2, -1], [-1, 2]])
eigs = np.linalg.eigvals(K)
mk.md(r"""
The element stiffness matrix is

{K}

with eigenvalues {eigs}.
""")
```

`{K}` is a 2×2 ndarray, so it renders as a markdown table. `{eigs}` is a
1-D ndarray and renders inline. Want LaTeX `bmatrix` instead? Wrap with
`ltx`:

```python
mk.md("The element stiffness matrix is {K_ltx}", K_ltx=ltx(K))
```

### A markdown table from a DataFrame

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({"node": [1, 2, 3], "u_x": [0.0, 1.2, 2.4]})
mk.md("""
## Nodal displacements

{df}

Total: {df.shape[0]} nodes.
""")
```

Quarto renders the table with its own CSS, applies cross-ref tags, etc.
Marimo renders the same markdown via its KaTeX/markdown pipeline. Same
input, two correct outputs.

## API sketch

```python
class Markdown:
    """Markdown source with dual rendering for quarto + marimo + jupyter."""
    def __init__(self, text: str):
        self.text = text
    def __str__(self) -> str:
        return self.text
    def _repr_markdown_(self) -> str:    # quarto / jupyter pick this up
        return self.text
    def _mime_(self):                    # marimo's preferred protocol
        return "text/markdown", self.text


def md(template: str) -> Markdown:
    frame = inspect.currentframe().f_back
    ns = {**frame.f_globals, **frame.f_locals}
    formatter = _MkFormatter(ns)         # eval-aware Formatter subclass
    return Markdown(formatter.vformat(template, (), {}))
```

`_MkFormatter` overrides two methods. `get_field` resolves arbitrary Python
expressions (so `{F_st/1000}`, `{a.b[0]}`, `{f(x)}` all work). `format_field`
holds the type dispatcher:

```python
def format_field(self, value, spec):
    if spec:
        return format(value, spec)        # respect user's explicit spec
    return _default_render(value)         # type dispatch, table above
```

`_default_render` is a small `singledispatch` table — one branch per type in
the table above. Adding a new type later means one new dispatch arm, not a
new function. The wrapper is intentionally not a templating engine; it
just teaches `str.format` about MechanicsKit's data types.

## Escaping

`str.format` still owns `{` and `}`, so LaTeX braces double up — same rule
as the f-string version of the table example above (`$F_{{st}}$`,
`$R_{{p02}}$`):

```python
mk.md(r"$F = \dfrac{{1}}{{2}} {F1}$")
#                    ^^^   ^^^         literal { } in LaTeX
#                                ^^^^  variable F1
```

This is the cost of "no `f` prefix and named lookup". Worth it; document
loudly.

## Decisions locked in

- **Name:** `mk.md`. (Yes, `md` is also "mass" in dynamics; users can
  shadow with `from mechanicskit import md as markdown` if it bites.)
- **Table threshold:** 8×8. ndarrays at or below that render as markdown
  tables; larger ones fall back to truncated LaTeX `bmatrix`, matching
  `LatexArray`'s existing truncation behavior.
- **Tables:** `tabulate` is a hard dependency. One library serves
  ndarray, `list[list]`, and DataFrame paths uniformly — pandas'
  `df.to_markdown()` is also `tabulate` underneath, so this aligns the
  whole table story on one engine.
- **Figures:** out of scope. Figures stay in their own cells/chunks.

## Behavior on unrecognized types and missing variables

Match `mo.md` / f-string semantics: lenient on type, strict on lookup.

- A placeholder whose value falls through the type table (`None`, a
  dataclass, a Sympy `Expr` we haven't taught it about, anything) renders
  as `str(value)`. That is what an f-string would do, and it is what
  marimo's f-string-based pipeline already does today.
- A placeholder whose **name** can't be resolved in the caller's
  namespace raises a clear `NameError` with the missing name. Silent
  failure on undefined variables is the kind of thing that wastes a
  lecture.

## Format-spec hooks

Skipped in v1. No `{x:math}`, no `{x:matrix}`. Standard format specs
(`{x:0.2f}`, `{x:>10}`) keep working because `_MkFormatter.format_field`
falls through to Python's `format(value, spec)` for any spec it doesn't
recognize — and after this decision, it doesn't recognize any.

If a user wants LaTeX composition, they call `ltx(...)` and interpolate
its result:

```python
mk.md("Stiffness: {expr}", expr=ltx(r"K = ", K, r"\,N/m"))
```

That keeps the API surface flat: one verb per concept (`md` for
markdown, `ltx` for LaTeX, `la` for arrays). No new mini-language to
learn inside `{...}`.

## Out of scope (for v1)

- Cross-references, citations, figure numbering — these are quarto's job
  and our markdown output already passes through to it.
- Custom directives (`{.callout-note}` etc.) — those are just markdown,
  users can write them in the template and they'll work in quarto.
- Caching / memoization — the per-call cost is negligible.
